titleStmt.title: The Chargé in Egypt ( (Jacobs) ) to the Secretary of State

div.head: The Chargé in Egypt ( Jacobs ) to the Secretary of State
div.p: 1534. Cairo’s 1513, June 3, 6 p.m.Not printed. For Murray from Moose.

list.item: Discussions of joint American-British assistance for Saudi Arabia have been confined to economic considerations though political factors have obviously determined attitude on both sides. American aim has been to devise supply and financial program adequate to maintain order and stability of Government of Saudi Arabia with ultimate objective of insuring Saudi independence and of protecting existing American interests. British on other hand have been so determined to effect drastic reduction not only in British subsidy but in all Saudi expenditures that they seem to consider Saudi stability as of secondary importance. Coupled with other projects originated or encouraged by Jordan since February it appears that he at any rate hopes to establish and exercise effective control over Saudi Government.
Any economic discussion which Landis may have in Washington should keep in mind fundamental political nature of Saudi problem and importance of oil interests involved.

British say latest proposals referred to London sympathetically. They promised to show Landis telegram to London before sending it but failed to do so.A copy of the telegram was given to Landis; see telegram 1563, June 8, 8 p.m., from Cairo, infra. …

Proposals represent large concession to British insistence on economy made in effort to secure joint program. In my opinion additional assistance may have to be provided before end of year. Proposed program to some extent based on confidential information supplied by British which could not be verified. Data from Saudi official sources entirely different.

Jordan says British Treasury opposes further silver for Saudi Arabia on grounds of inflationary tendencies and has so advised “Washington”. Please advise if Department has been informed of
this attitude. Jordan objects to more lend lease silver because when Saudi Arabia agrees to return silver United States has Saudi Arabia “in a cleft stick” meaning United States can use indebtedness to apply pressure on Saudi Arabia. This would seem to be a danger only when United States is creditor because Jordan proposed to King Ibn Saud a sterling loan secured by 1944 pilgrimage revenues and British experts here revived suggestion of sterling loan to cover 1944 budget deficit.

British say all their advances to Saudi Arabia are unconditional and that no repayment is expected though Saudi Government not informed. This is new development since hitherto advances have been described as loans in receipts taken by British Legation in Jidda.

Since Jordan opposed lend lease silver for Saudi Arabia and even attempted to persuade King and Saudi officials not to accept such silver, failure of the United States to supply at least part of the 20 million riyals already requested will be construed locally as proof that British policy overrules American policy. Such impression would be most unfortunate.

British authorities have not disavowed military mission, road mission or financial adviser sponsored or encouraged by Jordan in competition with American plans known to him nor have they shown any alacrity in reaching agreement on military and economic missions. It is therefore suggested that until the full scope and degree of American-British cooperation in Saudi Arabia is determined the Department continue with the Treasury’s plans for currency reform and central bank preferably with British participation but if necessary without. [Moose.]
